question: How do you handle failures in distributed systems?

content.explanation: In enterprise production systems, "How do you handle failures in distributed systems?" frequently appears during architecture reviews, on-call incidents, and senior-level interviews. Interviewers expect you to connect theory with trade-offs: consistency vs availability, latency vs throughput, and operational cost vs developer velocity. When answering, structure your response as definition → internal mechanism → real-world example → pitfalls → when not to use it. For Microservices / Distributed Systems, cite how teams at scale validate behavior using metrics, integration tests, and staged rollouts. Mention observability (logs, traces, dashboards) and safe rollout patterns (feature flags, canary, blue-green). This demonstrates staff-engineer thinking beyond textbook definitions.
content.internalWorking: At runtime, "How do you handle failures in distributed systems?" maps to concrete behavior in the Microservices stack. Understand which layer owns the work (client, service, framework, database, broker) and what guarantees apply (latency, ordering, durability). Trace a single request path and identify bottlenecks using profiling and metrics.
content.realTimeUseCase: Teams apply this in payment pipelines, inventory services, and high-traffic APIs where Distributed Systems decisions affect uptime. Use feature flags, staged rollouts, and automated tests before changing behavior in production.
performanceConsiderations: Cache hot paths with TTL invalidation
content.summary: Master "How do you handle failures in distributed systems?" by explaining fundamentals, production impact, and trade-offs in Microservices / Distributed Systems.
